title: 1x12 Bookcase with Wainscoting Back
summary: A basic 1x12 bookcase dressed up with a beadboard back and mitered footer trim — the back panel does double duty as decoration, since it shows through the open sides.
description:
The bookcase itself is the simplest part: two sides and two shelves, screwed together with the outside edges kept flush. What elevates it is the beadboard back standing in for plain plywood — since this bookcase has no face frame or door hiding the interior, the back panel is visible from the front through the whole depth of the case, so its material choice matters more here than on a closed cabinet.

The footer trim is mitered from base moulding stock or shaped with a router and an ogee bit if you want to profile it yourself — either approach works, and buying pre-shaped base moulding saves the router setup if you don't already have the right bit. Miter the footer's corners at the shortest points as noted in the cut list, which keeps the visible reveal consistent even though the pieces meet at an angle rather than square.

tools.note: Optional, for shaping your own footer profile with an ogee bit instead of buying pre-shaped base moulding.

materials.name: Pine, 1x4, 6 ft
materials.note: Or base moulding, pre-shaped, if not routing your own footer profile.

cutList.note: Mitered at the shortest points — router an ogee profile or use pre-shaped 4" base moulding.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the 10 ft 1x12s cut two sides at 32-1/2", two shelves at 40", and the 44-1/2" front tabletop; those five pieces come off two of the three supplied boards, so you have a spare board if a cut goes wrong. From the 1x3 cut the 40" front top trim. From the single 12 ft 1x2 cut the 40" footer support and the 44-1/2" back tabletop trim. From the 6 ft 1x4 cut the 41-1/2" front footer and two 11-1/2" side footers — that fills the board, so cut the long piece first. Trim the beadboard to 41-1/2" wide by 32-1/2" tall. Hold off mitering the three footer pieces until the case is built and you can measure it, since the miters are cut to the finished carcass.

steps.title: Attach the shelves to the sides
steps.body: The two 32-1/2" sides run the full height and the two 40" shelves sit between them, which makes the case 41-1/2" wide overall — the beadboard back is cut 41-1/2" by 32-1/2" and only closes on that reading, so do not build it the other way round. Set the bottom shelf flush with the bottom ends of the sides and put the second shelf wherever you want the opening, marking both sides together so it lands level. Fasten through the outside face of each side into the shelf ends with screws and glue, keeping the outside edges flush. Measure both diagonals and adjust before the glue sets.

steps.title: Add the front top trim
steps.body: The 40" 1x3 front top trim spans between the two sides at the top — the same length as the shelves, because it sits in the same place they do. Set its top edge flush with the top ends of the sides and its face flush with their front edges, then glue it and pin through each side into its ends with 1-1/4" brads. Check the diagonals once more here: with the back not yet on, this piece is the only thing holding the top of the case at 40", and whatever shape it sets is the shape the beadboard has to fit.

steps.title: Attach the beadboard back
steps.body: Lay the case face down and set the 41-1/2" by 32-1/2" beadboard over the back. It is cut to the case's full outside footprint, so its edges land on the side and shelf edges rather than sitting inside them. Because this bookcase has no doors and no closed sides, the back is visible through the whole depth of every shelf, so line the beads up square to the case before a single nail goes in. Tack one corner, check the diagonals, then nail off through the panel into both sides and both shelves with 1-1/4" brads at roughly 6" spacing.

steps.title: Add the footer
steps.body: Fasten the 40" 1x2 footer support across the front at the bottom, under the bottom shelf, with its front edge flush — this is what the mitered front footer lands on and it is far easier to fit now than to reach later. Then cut the footer itself: the front piece 41-1/2" and the two side pieces 11-1/2", all mitered at their shortest points, meaning the length given is the short face and the miter runs long from there outward. Route an ogee into the top edge yourself, or use pre-shaped 4" base moulding and skip the router setup entirely. Dry-fit all three pieces together before any glue, then attach with 1-1/4" brads.

steps.title: Add the tabletop pieces
steps.body: Fasten the 44-1/2" 1x2 back tabletop trim across the back edge of the case top first, then lay the 44-1/2" by 11-1/4" front tabletop over the top. Both pieces are 3" longer than the 41-1/2" case, so centre them and you get a 1-1/2" overhang at each end — measure both overhangs before nailing, because an uneven one is obvious from across the room. Glue and nail down with 1-1/4" brads into the sides and the front top trim.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand every surface and edge, working up through the grits in the assorted pack and easing the sharp corners on the tabletop and the footer. Get into the beadboard grooves with folded paper by hand — the orbital sander will not reach them and will only round over the bead. Then finish, and do the beadboard back first while you can still reach it easily; it is the visible feature of this piece and the hardest surface to get at once the bookcase is against a wall.

images.alt: 1x12 bookcase with a beadboard wainscoting back
